attack_chain.description: Attacker exploited the Shellshock (Bash) vulnerability on an unpatched, inactive prototype EC2 machine that had been running since before 2012.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: It was penetrated using the shellshock vulnerability, and since it was no longer in active use, it did not have the appropriate patch installed.

evidence_chunks.quote: It was penetrated using the shellshock vulnerability, and since it was no longer in active use, it did not have the appropriate patch installed.
It was penetrated using the shellshock vulnerability, and since it was no longer in active use, it did not have the appropriate patch installed.

attack_chain.description: Attacker harvested long-lived AWS API access key and secret key stored on the compromised prototype machine.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The old prototype machine had our AWS API access key and secret key.

evidence_chunks.quote: The old prototype machine had our AWS API access key and secret key.
The old prototype machine had our AWS API access key and secret key.

attack_chain.description: Using the stolen AWS keys, the attacker created a new IAM user and generated an EC2 key-pair for persistent access to the AWS account.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: he created an IAM user, and generated a key-pair

evidence_chunks.quote: he created an IAM user, and generated a key-pair
he created an IAM user, and generated a key-pair

attack_chain.technique_name: Modify Cloud Compute Infrastructure: Create Cloud Instance
attack_chain.description: Attacker launched a new EC2 instance inside the BrowserStack AWS account using the attacker-controlled credentials.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: He was then able to run an instance inside our AWS account using these credentials

evidence_chunks.quote: He was then able to run an instance inside our AWS account using these credentials
He was then able to run an instance inside our AWS account using these credentials

attack_chain.technique_name: Modify Cloud Compute Infrastructure: Revert Cloud Instance
attack_chain.description: Attacker mounted one of BrowserStack's backup disks (EBS snapshot) to the attacker-controlled instance to access production configuration data.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: mount one of our backup disks. This backup was of one of our component services, used for production environment, and contained a config file with our database password.

evidence_chunks.quote: mount one of our backup disks. This backup was of one of our component services, used for production environment, and contained a config file with our database password.
mount one of our backup disks. This backup was of one of our component services, used for production environment, and contained a config file with our database password.

attack_chain.description: Attacker read the mounted backup's config file to obtain the production database password.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: contained a config file with our database password

evidence_chunks.quote: contained a config file with our database password
contained a config file with our database password

attack_chain.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Firewall
attack_chain.description: Attacker modified the database security group (AWS firewall) to whitelist their own IP address, enabling direct connection to the production DB.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: He also whitelisted his IP on our database security group, which is the AWS firewall.

evidence_chunks.quote: He also whitelisted his IP on our database security group, which is the AWS firewall.
He also whitelisted his IP on our database security group, which is the AWS firewall.

attack_chain.description: Attacker connected to the production database and began copying a user table containing email IDs, bcrypt-hashed passwords, and last tested URL. The copy operation locked the table, triggering monitoring alerts.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: He began to copy one of our tables, which contained partial user information, including email IDs, hashed passwords, and last tested URL. His copy operation locked the database table, which raised alerts on our monitoring system.

evidence_chunks.quote: He began to copy one of our tables, which contained partial user information, including email IDs, hashed passwords, and last tested URL. His copy operation locked the database table, which raised alerts on our monitoring system.

He began to copy one of our tables, which contained partial user information, including email IDs, hashed passwords, and last tested URL. His copy operation locked the database table, which raised alerts on our monitoring system.

attack_chain.description: Attacker exfiltrated a portion of the user-table data before defenders blocked the unrecognized source IP.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: In that time, the hacker had been able to retrieve only a portion of the data.

evidence_chunks.quote: In that time, the hacker had been able to retrieve only a portion of the data.
In that time, the hacker had been able to retrieve only a portion of the data.

attack_chain.description: Using the exfiltrated email addresses and the victim's SES credentials, the attacker sent a phishing/hoax email (claiming BrowserStack was shutting down) to approximately 5,000 BrowserStack users.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: using this data and the SES credentials, he was able to send an email to some of our users.

evidence_chunks.quote: using this data and the SES credentials, he was able to send an email to some of our users.
using this data and the SES credentials, he was able to send an email to some of our users.

initial_access_vector: Exploitation of the Shellshock (CVE-2014-6271) vulnerability on an unpatched, inactive prototype EC2 machine exposed to the internet.
persistence_mechanism: Attacker created a new IAM user and generated an EC2 key-pair in the victim's AWS account to maintain access independent of the compromised prototype host.
privilege_escalation: Harvested long-lived AWS API access/secret keys stored on the compromised host, granting full AWS account privileges far exceeding those of the local prototype server.
